item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as no source found indicates it has been released under Creative Commons. Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
love the twist, pretty ahead of its time imo!

1+

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this would be excellent as a Manna donation. I can see how their good intentions and lax testing might pose a serious problem to both the Manna Foundation and the SCP Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Liked it, but I find it strange Foundation would fall for it for any meaningful amount of time. They deal with cognitohazards on daily basis and they would have procedures to prevent that.

And yah, a little too stuble.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At first I thought this thing caused a severe case of "being american" but then I read the spoilers. Luckily, because I would have never figured it out on my own. My point is that this scip is too subtle. I hate when creepy things are made obvious because it kills the atmosphere, but making them so obscure that you don't notice them until someone else specifically points them out to you leaves you to think that there is no atmosphere at all. If I hadn't read the comments I would have thought that this scip was a good idea with a poor execution, but now I know it is.

Downvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So basically this is self replicating McDonalds?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ehhhh. I could like a never-ending table of food, and I could like an SCP where you starve because you don't realise you're not eating (the concept was magnificent t in Good Omens) but I don't really like this.

For all its attempts to be a Fridge piece, it still feels like A Thing What Kills You If You Interact With It.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It took me a while to get the hint, but I really liked this one. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Going with that angle, two images could be added to the article, one of an empty table and then the one already there. Set up a script that logs unique ips for a 24 hour period. First hit for an ip, empty table picture. Subsequent viewings or refreshing, food picture.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But surely the mental differences between the two sorts of food, and how the body handles them, would be enough to seriously mess you up, physically? Imagine you'd been fed on imaginary food for a while. You -think- you're getting fed, getting nutrients. Then you actually have real food, and real nutrients. How would the body react?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Add in something a little less subtly mind-affecting. It can be a small thing, but make it such that it's not so great a leap to think the Foundation is being affected by the object. Ideally, the Foundation notices the small mind-affecting trait, and the reader to think, "Oh, but they're missing the huge one! Because they're affected!" You want that "oh shit" moment to strike them.

Right now, if it weren't for the mind-affecting tag, it would appear that the conclusion you're going for is that the Foundation is too stupid to recognize starvation when they see it. You need to give that extra nudge to bring the reader where you want them without seeing these comments or the tags.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was exaggerating the results slightly; they're operating with a tiny sample size. I take your point, though, and I have been erring on the side of obscure over obvious. Do you have any particular suggestions other than adjusting the refeeding stuff?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Which doesn't match the facts here. Refeeding syndrome puts you at risk for a heart attack, but it isn't something that occurs in 100% of all cases. It can be fatal. It isn't always, and it shouldn't be killing them in most of the cases (at least, not at first; cycles of starvation and refeeding would likely make it a certainty).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Death by heart attack is actually a big hint.

http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Refeeding_syndrome

" Cardiac arrhythmias are the most common cause of death from refeeding syndrome,"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mm. I don't think the mind-affecting bit really comes through that well. If you can find a way to get that across better, it would help make the Foundation look less credulous (because it would help the reader connect the dots and see how the Foundation's being affected). Assume your reader can't even see the tags. Find another way to get that idea across.

I'd get rid of the bit about other food giving them heart attacks. They already strongly prefer the food from 1117. As it is, they're dead whether they try to subsist on the food or not. By having normal food kill them, you lose a lot of the horror, insofar as they're doing it to themselves and don't realize it. If you have normal food kill them, it loses that bit of horror.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This went a bit better.. but still, lack of energy isn't a scientific description for a reason a person dies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At least the bodies take up less room.

Welp, that's an upvote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
With these new additions the article just gets even sweeter; I really love the idea it brings across, which is that - despite the foundation's impressive resources and medical knowledge, in the end, it can be trumped by something as simple as starvation.
It's brilliant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Tweaked with a few suggestions. Not sure if the addendum works, input welcome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Too subtle, but good once you know what it really is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice and tricky. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And that's specifically why i check announcements/most recently created before i go to forum posts ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And then they all die, but not before the rest of the world knows about it and starts using it. This thing could very well cause the end of the world.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Clever SCP. Sadly I'd read some spoiler comments via recent posts so the effect was muted for me, but love the concept.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is very good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's rather the point of the item that you don't get what's going on with it until you're in a bath two days later.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm ambivalent about this. Reading spoilers made the item more interesting, but it's not pointed out clearly enough for my liking. No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Tablecloth found to have no anomalous properties." Not the food that the image is showing; the tablecloth just underneath it, that we can't see because of the food that isn't there. I didn't get this at first, but shit, is that cute, and there's enough in the article that you can go back and see where you could've figured it out. The path of least resistance here as far as "hiding" the physiological effects of starvation would just be SCP magic, but there's probably something better to come up with there. Either way I think this works as is. Upvoting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Basilisk, I think you're running into the line between completely internally consistent scp laws and conveying the punchline clearly. As has been said—illusory food, that also disguises your appearance, that /also shows up on film/ may be scary, but there are hardly any clues that you're supposed to be scared.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Looking forward to reading the improvements :) There's definitely something worthwhile here: Just needs a little love!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Illusory food which prevents the eaters from realizing they're starving to death is cool. Illusory food which also prevents everyone else from realizing they're starving to death is going too far, in my opinion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel conflicted. I really like the idea of the object, and feel okay about the writing. But I hate it when the Foundation uses SCP's for practical purposes. Yes, I understand that there are a lot of items, especially the older ones, that do this. I think that is mostly a relic of when they are written, and that the wiki is better served by moving away from that idea.
My suggestion? Place the note about the use being somewhere backdated, as though that had been an earlier Foundation policy that had been discontinued. Even better, use this as an example why that policy had been thrown out.

But I can't really offer a reason to get me to vote on the article as is. Just different tastes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think what he's going for is a sort of memetic effect, more or less. The food doesn't exist, anyone with telekill would know it. Though perhaps the camera thing is a good idea, I'm not very familiear with memetics quite yet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I sorta like it as-is, and I'm sure I'll like it more once it's touched up further. I'll go ahead and +1 it now so I don't forget later ;)

(BTW, I'm so entirely going to make a fake-Feast-Table item in my next D&D campaign that does this)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ok, that works very nicely. I'll type that up when I've got some time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just to avoid spoilers
– hide block

I just imagine that once you've seen the food, you'll always see it, even in images. So someone who hasn't encountered the SCP would see nothing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Perhaps have it say that their condition worsened 'with each instance' of SCP-1117 that was consumed?
The Foundation isn't Omniscient, after all, and it could obviously come to this conclusion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think it'd work best if the article were to say that 'with each individual instance of SCP-1117-2 consumed' this effect worsened, to help put the reader off finding the schtick too easily.

edit: damn, this post didn't show up initially. Sorry for the DP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That is the tricky question - making it reasonable while not spoiling the fridge horror element. I'll definitely think carefully about improvements.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait a tick, how large is it's area of effect? wouldn't a camera or other recording device pick up on this? what about the average public? what caries the effect? is it the food or the table?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmm, but the mindscrew would have to alter appearance, mass, as well as things like clothes size which seems simple but is hard when you think about it.

what might work better as a mental effect is not that their appearance is not noticeable, but that noone gives a damn.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the idea of the food not actually being there: Reminds me of fae lore, which I adore.

However, I think that rather than the affectees dropping dead with no signs of ill health, it could be much more effective if their health and appearance deteriorated. Since they would be dying of starvation, then it follows that they must have used up their energy reserves and would have been burning fat.

I do appreciate that you're trying to occlude this fact from being immediately recognisable, though. I just feel that cause and effect could be more closely linked.

Perhaps the table projects some kind of glamour, or mental block to observers, repealing it only upon the subject's death?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I threw a hint into the tags. Mind-affecting and cognitohazard, on killer food? Hmm… must be something going on here…

Though not everyone looks at tags, I don't a lot of the time.

Hmm… I like the picture idea, I'll think about that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The mindscrew continues after death - sure, if they got in someone immune to the effects (inb4telekillrage) to do the autopsy, they'd see all the signs. But as it is…

Vaelynx: given the long period before the effects would really show up, I figured that they might put it to limited use, not having seen any issues. The whole monthly termination thing would prevent them from spotting the issue with the Ds. It's a slight stretch, but I can see it getting put through by some bored scientist to try out on one or two people, before the oshi- moment. On your other point, the human body is capable of some pretty impressive stuff - if they literally don't know there's anything wrong with them, are incapable of perceiving it, I think it quite likely that they would be able to make themselves keep going by sheer (if unconscious) force of will - eventually that gives out, leading to the fatigue effect near death.

If you actually got to see the body without the mindscrew effect (inb4telekill), I imagine it would be pretty stomach-churning.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
OH.

Yeah, too subtle. I was thinking Keter cakes at first, and then "it just kills you" killed my upvote. I think I'll +1 it now.

+ show block
– hide block

That said, why does the food show up on camera? I'd recommend using the image as your hook, a picture of an empty table with "SCP-1117, laden with various foods" as the caption to let the reader know something's up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think it's about good now; the container thing would be kind of weird.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: makes sense, though wouldn't the autopsy show that they died of starvation?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it. But, to avoid the confusion that seems to prevalent, perhaps you should consider making that a tad more explicit. Or not, actually. Others may catch on faster than I did.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this a typical case of people not 'getting the joke' as it were.
Best fridge horror entry i've read for a while. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

Thing is, they show no symptoms of starvation or even lack of food. They might not be hungry, but the body fed by something lacking nutrition would react accordingly. Heart attack after a month of being fine is not the reaction I imagine.

Also, why would the foundation approve it for use by personnel before exploring long-term effects?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Took me a bit to catch on to it, but I'm glad I did. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Spoiler
- Hide

Correct. Did some research into starvation statistics - times of death are roughly correct, assuming healthy individuals.

The table is actually empty. The whole effect is simply fooling everyone around into thinking that nothing's wrong. People eventually starve to death - or in the case of Class Ds in the addendum, get serious refeeding syndrome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The food doesn't actually exist?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Editted. During concept development, I had it in mind for the -3s to receive a container of some kind that effectively followed them around that they could draw endless food supplies from - do you think that would be a better mechanic?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Quite the opposite. I'll give you a hint: the food is not a problem. In fact, if they ate other food as well, they'd be perfectly fine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, you should. is it like, they stare at an empty table and it appears before their eyes? Or do they open a cabinet and it's filled with scpfood? Also, how do you tell the difference between scp food and normal versions of the same?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't get the hook here, it's a murderous meal? maybe you could work in a way that the meal's victims become integrated in the next meal? "this meatloaf tastes familiar, what's this sauce? hey.. is that a…. OH GOD CHRIST! IT'S FUCKING JIMMY!!!" that sort of thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't think this is such a bad idea. If SCP-1117-2 can manifest anywhere near SCP-1117-3 then it has the potential to reach pandemic proportions within somewhat limited time. Imagine a six course meal manifesting among a bunch of starving villagers in the third world.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: All the clues are in there for what's actually happening - though I may have been a little too subtle.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Do the victims die for a particular reason, or just so you know it means business?
